narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there. Your background includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.40, an LSAT score of 29.50, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your Year 1 school decile ranking of 2 and Year 3 decile of 1. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 76.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam—far higher than the lower risks seen in approved applicants—leading to the tough decision to reject your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing, strongly pushing the model toward expecting failure. Your Year 1 decile, also quite low relative to averages, reinforced this concern as the next biggest factor pulling in the same direction. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, though below the dataset average, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA had a mixed influence but ultimately contributed toward the failure prediction, while your upper-middle family income added a small push in the unfavorable direction, unlike some students from varied backgrounds who fare better. Your choice to go full-time aligned with the vast majority of students (over 90%) and provided a minor boost toward passing. Compared to typical students—who often have higher decile rankings around the 5th or 6th out of 10, LSAT scores near 37, and GPAs around 3.2—your academic deciles stood out as unusually low, overshadowing other strengths and tipping the scales. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic paths, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement in later years, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and perhaps exploring programs that value holistic profiles. We encourage you to seek mentorship from successful Black lawyers, consider community college or alternative pathways to build your record, and reapply once you've addressed these key areas—you have the resilience to succeed. Keep pushing forward; your story is still being written.
